_README: Hand-authored STRUCTURE for the generated history (docs/history.html). The generator (tools/build-history.js) derives ALL dates, commit lists, ADRs and cart-birth data from git + index.json — this file only supplies what a parser can't infer: era boundaries, subsystem identity + matchers, importance tiers, the marked days, and the (mostly empty) editorial seam. Editorial prose, when written, follows docs/guides/voice.md. Re-run: `node tools/build-history.js`. v1 scope = week 1 only.
title: dreamengine — a history
subtitle: How a fantasy console grew, week by week. Built structure-first from git, the STATUS ledger, and the decision log.

eras.title: Bootstrap — "get a window open"
eras.blurb: Ported from the sibling 'eventually' studio and renamed to dreamengine. One evening: editor chrome (Makefile, clipboard, tabs), assets embedded straight into the binary, a Windows cross-compile — capped at 23:55 by a single 'big batch' commit that drops the first sound system and the map editor.

eras.title: The API sprint
eras.blurb: A research braindump (Scratch / Strudel / Dilla) feeds two days of rapid-fire primitives and helpers. The cart pipeline forms (make-cart, tutorials, save/load), the web build lands (emscripten + a Build-for-Web button), and the first real synth arrives (ADSR / duty / LFO / resonant filter). The docs get reorganised by genre and the decision log is born.

eras.title: Rigor & tooling
eras.blurb: Less new surface, more correctness. Camera transforms, arc/ring primitives, and a hard rasterization-consistency push (one coverage test for circ/oval/tri/rrect). The debug harness lands — deterministic record/replay/script/trace. Fifty carts arrive at once via parallel Workflow batches. Geometry helpers, physics sketches, and fade() made immediate-mode.

eras.title: Subsystems are born
eras.blurb: The pivotal stretch. In two days: the profiler, dreamcad (a 3D model editor), held notes (note_on/off), modrack (a modular synth, steps 0→6, plus a module zoo), and the libtcc live backend (cart-as-script, de_state, hot-reload). The navkit instrument-port spec is written — it seeds the entire June sound saga — and mod-envelopes give the synth its second EG.

eras.title: Sound tools & the first stations
eras.blurb: The SFX editor and slider-driven generator land, plus drawable single-cycle waveforms (wave_set / INSTR_USER) and sample-accurate scheduling (schedule_hit). The first generative radio stations appear — bossa, ambient, jangle, lowend — and the cart taxonomy gets its capstones (the collision, movement and steering clusters). A dropped-request tripwire makes the sound queue trustworthy.

eras.title: Roland machines & the radio explosion
eras.blurb: The densest day yet. The Roland drum-machine family is synthesized from measured analog circuit values — CR-78, TR-808, TB-303, TR-909 — and a dozen radio stations pour out (city pop, dub, house, satie, tango, exotica, yacht, roadhouse, cocktail, ymo). radio.h is extracted as the shared station chassis, the first navkit port engines arrive (PLUCK, MALLET, FM), drive and echo become the first bus effects, and THE BAND panel + the improviser (improv.h) give the stations players.

eras.title: Mobile, widgets & the first keyboards
eras.blurb: A breather, then tooling. touch_ceiling() and the mobile worklist land, plus a debug overlay. ui.h ships a cross-input widget kit — button/slider/knob for mouse, touch and keyboard at once. The first keyboard instruments arrive (INSTR_ORGAN, INSTR_EPIANO) alongside teaching carts: flappy, the smallest complete game, and whatiscode, lesson zero.

eras.title: The engine-port marathon
eras.blurb: The navkit instrument ports land in a row until every modeled timbre exists: the envelope follower (a third modulation source), PD, REED (blown waveguide), PIPE (jet flute), GUITAR + PIANO (verbatim StifKarp), BOWED (Smith/McIntyre stick-slip), and finally BRASS — the last engine. Audio goes stereo (instrument_pan / LFO_PAN), the solo.h jam layer lets the player solo over a station, and the formant filter is specced.

eras.title: The effects cookbook & true colour
eras.blurb: The §8.10 effects roster completes: per-instrument chorus/flanger (effects on one instrument, not the whole mix), tape (wow/flutter/saturation), and the long-promised auto-wah — the 'scar' finally resolved as a bus effect. Drive modes and bitcrush ship too. On the graphics side, true-colour pixels arrive — pset_rgb / rectfill_rgb / pget_rgb — powering CPU shader carts.

eras.title: The mixing console & MIDI
eras.blurb: The engine becomes a real mixing console: a reorderable insert chain (fx_order), a multi-reverb tank pool, sidechain + bus compression (glue), a swept filter, and auto-pan. Then it learns to listen — engine-level MIDI keyboard input (CoreMIDI native, Web MIDI in the browser) plus keybed.h, a shared polyphonic keybed seven carts migrate onto. Audits (mono/poly, keycap) and the spline road world (roadnet) round out the stretch.

eras.title: Spatial audio
eras.blurb: Positional audio in two layers, one day. v1: a listener() + positioned voices give automatic pan, distance-volume and Doppler (the OpenAL model in 2D pixel space). v2: emitter buses move a whole effected source as one object, so its reverb/shimmer tail pans and Dopplers with it. The library also crosses its scale milestones — 14 MACRO engines, 32 simultaneous voices.

eras.title: Roads, sims & engine tuning
eras.blurb: Attention turns to the big sims and the long tail of polish. The deterministic spline road world grows up — roadlab (lane-accurate roads + clothoid joints), interchange (a data-driven junction matrix), rampkit — and flank, a tactical stealth-squad game, takes shape. On the audio side, the waveguide top-octave tuning is fixed (PLUCK/REED/BRASS) and a generalized all-engine tuner lands.

markedDays.2026-05-28: The bootstrap night — a whole studio stood up in one evening, from initial commit to a midnight 'big batch'.
markedDays.2026-06-02: The big bang — modrack AND the libtcc live backend both born on the same day, with held-notes and dreamcad alongside.
markedDays.2026-06-05: The radio explosion — a dozen stations and the whole Roland drum-machine family born in one day, plus radio.h and the first port engines.
markedDays.2026-06-10: The engine program finishes — INSTR_BRASS lands as the last modeled timbre, so every instrument the library needs now exists.
markedDays.2026-06-12: The mixing console day — the effects bus becomes reorderable (fx_order) with multi-reverb and sidechain, and MIDI keyboards play the engine (CoreMIDI + Web MIDI + keybed.h).
markedDays.2026-06-15: Spatial audio — listener + positioned voices, then whole emitter buses that move (Doppler + a shimmer tail that travels with the source), shipped in one day.

threads._README: RESEARCH THREADS — topics that spawned a trail of docs, handoffs and carts. Declared by topic (like `subsystems`): the generator auto-collects every doc/ADR whose filename matches a `match` term, splits them into notes/handoffs/decisions, unions the carts that cite them (via the strong-signal scan), and computes the date span. AUTOMATIC: a new matching doc/handoff/cart folds in on the next `node tools/build-history.js` with no editing. The only hand-authored part is `title` + the voiced `blurb` (follows guides/voice.md); leave `blurb` empty and a structural sentence is derived instead.

items.title: Roads & a world to drive on
items.blurb: This one started small and would not stop. The idea was just to vary procplaces' roads-and-cities generator — split density into land-use and intensity, kill the grid look. Then it hit the wall: arced roads. You can't bend a road on a global grid without everything downstream quietly lying about where it goes. So roadnet threw the grid out for a node-lattice of splines — local, deterministic, infinite, honest. That cracked it open, and then it just kept going: a street level under the map (the fill language in lotfill), real junctions (interchange), the crowd that walks it (menigte). And sloop — the cart that actually drives the thing — is the whole reason it had to be true and not faked. Four handoffs deep, still open. The longest thread in the project, and the one most likely to eat another month.

items.title: The instrument-engine ports
items.blurb: Almost every voice in here is a port of navkit, done one at a time until the whole modeled orchestra existed — pluck, reed, the jet-flute pipe, guitar and piano straight off StifKarp, the bowed string, and brass last. The rule we learned the hard way: port the oscillator verbatim, don't "improve" it on the way in — every shortcut showed up later as a tuning scar (the waveguides go flat at the top of the keyboard; still chasing that one). Four handoffs and a dedicated tuner cart are the receipts. Trust your ears over the label — the doc says Rhodes, the ear says no, the ear wins.

items.title: The effects bus
items.blurb: The engine quietly turned into a mixing console. It started as 'effects are recipes, not primitives' — don't ship a reverb knob, ship the settings that make a space — and then the bus grew teeth: drive, echo, tape, the auto-wah that got built wrong once and torn out and redone right. The big call was making effects per-instrument and reorderable instead of one master strip, so a chorus can sit on the Rhodes alone and the order of the chain is yours. The boutique-pedals roadmap is the wishlist that keeps feeding it.

items.title: The radio stations
items.blurb: The stations are the part that plays itself. Each one is a genre studied blind first — write the ideal band from the music, before reading how the last cart did it, so the library doesn't homogenize — then built: a seeded song, a chord brain, voices picked to match. The blind-briefs are that first honest sketch; the genre-fidelity ledger is the brutal follow-up that says where we faked an instrument or skipped a brain. Bossa, dub, house, satie, a dozen more. The dream underneath is tinydaws — radios as song generators you can actually edit.

items.title: Mobile & touch
items.blurb: A desktop toy meeting a phone, and the phone winning a lot of the arguments. Real device testing turned up the stuff you can't reason about from a chair — the five-touch iPhone ceiling, phantom touches, fingers that swipe-tap-do-five-things-and-crash-it. Out of that came the loupe (a magnifier so you can actually grab a tiny knob), the shared widget kit so every cart handles mouse, touch and keyboard at once, and a couple of audits that swept every cart for whether a phone could even play it. Not glamorous. Load-bearing.

items.title: Spatial audio
items.blurb: Sound in space, done in two passes in basically a day. First the OpenAL move: a listener, voices with positions, automatic pan and distance-volume and Doppler in flat 2D pixel space. Then the better idea — move a whole effected source as one object, so its reverb tail and shimmer travel with it instead of staying nailed to the center. Small thread, but it's the one that makes a passing car sound like a passing car.

decisionNotes._README: Voiced one-liners for the 'Roads not taken' cards (and any decision worth a readable line), following guides/voice.md — replaces the dry ADR context paragraph on the page. Keyed by ADR basename without .md. No entry = fall back to the ADR's first-paragraph summary.
decisionNotes.0001-cut-coroutine-process-model: The DIV process model — the thing we'd put on the box as what PICO-8 doesn't have — got cut in week one. A coroutine per game object sounds great until you're writing the C-side scheduler instead of the game. Killed it before it could set the shape of everything after it.
decisionNotes.0008-cut-turtle-graphics-api: Shipped a whole Logo turtle, nine functions, then went looking for who used it. One cart. Out it came — a feature with one user isn't a feature, it's a maintenance bill.
decisionNotes.0013-cut-music-api: music() was the PICO-8 half of the sound plan: pattern banks, four channels, the works. Nobody ever called it — we'd already picked code-first music twice over. This is the exact fork where sound stopped being a tracker and started becoming a synth.
decisionNotes.0014-cut-unused-convenience-helpers: Three convenience helpers, zero carts using them, each the less-loved sibling of one that caught on. The usage audit found them; we cut them. Convenience nobody reaches for is just more surface to keep working.

observations._README: Cross-week RETROSPECTS — conclusions that only surface from reading the whole arc back at once, NOT legible commit-by-commit. Distinct from each era's `editorial` seam (which annotates one era from inside it); these look down on the entire window. Each `evidence` line is grounded in a number the generator can re-derive from git/index.json, so a future refresh can re-check it. Refresh by re-running the `recipe` against the current window and rewriting `items` + bumping `asOf`.
observations.title: Retrospect — reading the whole arc back
observations.recipe: How these were got, kept so it can be done again in due time: after the history page + spine are written, read them BACK as a finished whole — the rendered docs/history.html (which now mines design-doc CONTENT automatically: the per-era 'design-note spotlight' pulls each doc's friendly one-liner from docs/README.md + the carts that cite it, and 'Roads not taken' surfaces the cut ADRs), this spine, and the raw counts (`git log --since=<from> --date=short --pretty=%ad | sort | uniq -c` for per-day density; `grep -o '"subsystem": "[a-z0-9]*"' history-spine.json | sort | uniq -c` for the milestone mix; cart/ADR totals). Then ask the question that started it: "since we've written the docs/history code and text, can you now read it back and come to insights/conclusions you couldn't before?" Mine the negative space too — STATUS.md's 'Decided-against' ledger + the cut-* ADRs + the handoff docs (where work got parked) are as telling as what shipped. Rule: every claim cites a derived number, so it stays honest with the repo like the rest of the page. Caveat learned: per-day commit counts shift with timezone/day-boundary choices — cross-check before calling any single day a peak or a lull (the apparent '06-13 lull' was a date-grouping artifact).

items.title: It became a synthesizer with a screen
items.body: The stated identity is a learn-C fantasy console in the PICO-8 lineage. The revealed identity, in aggregate, is a software studio that happens to render graphics: 14 modeled instrument engines, a reorderable effects bus, MIDI, and positional audio. Each synth commit reads as 'just another cart' alone — only the pile shows sound ate the project. And this isn't only emergent: VISION.md and design/second-north-star.md name the shift outright ('a second pursuit has grown up alongside it… it now drives much of the work' — deep honest sims behind a lo-fi surface), so the git aggregate is confirming a turn the project already saw in itself.
items.evidence: 38 of ~60 milestones are audio (24 sound + 7 effects + 5 radio + 1 machines + 1 modrack ≈ 63%); 4 of the 6 marked days are pure audio; the pivot is documented in design/second-north-star.md (REFLECTION, 2026-06-09).

items.title: The fantasy console kept amputating its own fantasy-console features
items.body: The page celebrates what shipped, but the negative space is just as revealing: the project repeatedly removed PICO-8/DIV-style conveniences to keep the from-scratch lesson honest and code-first. The DIV coroutine/process model — billed in VISION as the headline differentiator from PICO-8 — was cut in WEEK ONE. Turtle graphics shipped, then was removed for one user. The music() pattern-bank was cut because the project had chosen code-first music twice over — which is the same decision that sent audio down the synthesis road (observation 1). Three zero-adoption helpers went after an API-usage audit. The throughline: subtract anything that lets the learner skip the real thing.
items.evidence: Four cut-ADRs: 0001 (coroutine model, cut 2026-05-30, week 1), 0008 (turtle graphics removed 2026-06-01), 0013 (music() → code-first, 2026-06-04), 0014 (3 unused helpers, 2026-06-04); plus STATUS.md's 'Decided-against / deferred' ledger.

items.title: One spec set three weeks' trajectory
items.body: The navkit instrument-port spec, written on 06-02 in the 'subsystems born' era, is the keystone. Trace it forward: the spec → the engine-port marathon (PLUCK→BRASS) → which created the demand for effects (all those voices need processing) → which created the mixing console (they need routing) → which created spatial audio (they need placing). Most of the last two weeks is downstream of one week-1 planning artifact, not one feature.
items.evidence: Spine flags it 'seeds the entire June sound saga'; the engine milestones it spawned span 06-05 (PLUCK) through 06-10 (BRASS, 'the last engine').

items.title: Throughput compounded — it never plateaued
items.body: The week-1 tooling (harness, profiler, libtcc live backend, make-cart + parallel Workflow batches, the radio.h/ui.h/keybed.h chassis) was the real product: weeks 2–3 are almost pure content riding that scaffolding. The marked-day note calls 06-05 'the densest day yet' — true when written, but four later days beat it. The investment paid off as accelerating output.
items.evidence: Per-day commits: week 1 runs 18–60; weeks 2–3 routinely break 100 (peaks 138, 129, 118, 113). 06-05 = 106. 391 carts total.

items.title: Every shared header is crystallized duplication — always in that order
items.body: The library headers under runtime/ never lead; they trail. radio.h was extracted after a dozen stations; keybed.h after seven keyboard carts; ui.h after hand-rolled widgets; pointer.h was retrofitted across the remaining hand-rolled-pool carts. ADR-0006 makes this deliberate — the engine doesn't own these, they precipitate out of cart-land once the pain is felt. Abstraction trails repetition every time.
items.evidence: radio.h 'extracted as the shared station chassis' (06-05); pointer.h 'adopt across the 13 remaining hand-rolled-pool carts' (06-13); keybed.h 'seven carts migrate onto' (console-midi era).

items.title: The audio system recapitulated the history of studio gear
items.body: It isn't one 'sound' blob — it's four strict layers, each presupposing the last: synthesis (instruments exist) → effects (instruments get processed) → console (instruments get mixed/routed/reordered) → spatial (instruments get placed in space). You can't have a mixing-console day until 14 engines and bus effects exist to mix; you can't place a signal until there's one worth placing. The order was emergent, and it matches how real studios evolved.
items.evidence: Era sequence: engine-marathon (synthesis) → fx-truecolor (effects) → console-midi (console) → spatial (placement), 06-08 through 06-15.

items.title: Long unresolved threads are only visible in aggregate
items.body: A single commit hides the threads that run across weeks. The auto-wah is described as 'the scar finally resolved as a bus effect' — implying an earlier parked attempt that sat unbuilt while a cheaper per-voice stand-in held the place. The engine marathon is the same shape: a goal declared early ('until every modeled timbre exists') and closed out only at BRASS. The whole-arc view is what surfaces these.
items.evidence: audio-notes.md still names a 'PARKED §8.10 bus wah' beside the per-voice cousin; auto-wah milestone resolves it in the fx-truecolor era (06-11).
